Now that we have some idea of what the heck a practitioner or a coach is, now I want to point out practitioners are insecure about their positioning amongst other practitioners. Practitioners are insecure about their positioning with clients. Clients are insecure about things that matter to them. You are here as a practitioner to ease and solve the client's pain, whatever that pain may be. People come to you because they already see you as an expert. Projecting your insecurities onto them only serves to unnecessarily complicate the already complex transformation process. So to put the beefy title on this thing, properly position yourself. The more you know, the less they care. They smack the microphone. This is, I'm passionate about this. So after stepping back and putting yourself in the shoes of the people in the middle, the people between you as a practitioner, other practitioners, you know, the client, the one that matters. One, people care if you can walk the walk. Two, people care if you can produce results. And number three, producing results is the same thing as walking the walk. All right. You don't have to be a supermodel to be a coach. You just have to respect yourself, respect the people you work with and produce results for those people. Number two is far more important than number one. Okay. People care if you can produce results. People care about this the most. Although generating an audience by being a public personality can have its perks. Personalist programs and businesses change lives every day. Huge big name brands that I don't need to name that don't have any specific person at the head that anybody knows about. Okay. The only difference lies in how the program is marketed. So for what it's worth, programs where people succeed, however they define success, those programs will grow. Programs where people fail, however they define it, those programs fade away into obscurity. When you know a lot about something that excites you, it's difficult to keep yourself from sharing it. Heck, that's why I've made this, right? It's easy to see how a person coming to you for advice maybe just as interested in what you know as you are. For that reason, most professionals are bursting at the seams with all the things they want to vomit onto their clients. All right. Maybe it's because the coach wants to see a lot of changes in a short period of time. Maybe it's because the coach feels like some basic level of education is required to implement their program. Maybe and commonly, the coach just wants to position themselves as an expert on the topic they're discussing. Throw all this fancy stuff at them. They'll think I'm smart, then the client will trust me. The point is that when people come to you for counsel, when people pay you for nutrition, fitness training or whatever, they already see you as an expert. If you continue to pound them with fancy verbiage and overly complicate the explanations of things that only require a sentence or two to implement, then it will only serve to confuse the client and make you look condescending or an a-hole. That's what that means. Is that condescending? I don't know. Okay. You don't need to prove how much you know. That's the point. They already know that you know more than them. That's why they came to you in the first place. I finally ended up realizing that I no longer needed to science people to death only to barely death, right? Barely living, barely beating them to within an... Okay. I believe to come to the conclusion that I no longer needed to over explain or rationalize my decisions unless I was prompted. Key words. Be an open book, but don't vomit on people. Many conceptual questions about your process, they're not stemming from a place of mistrust. A lot of people like, you're questioning me. Does that mean you don't trust me? And you get offended and that comes off on people. But rather for most people, they're coming from a place of curiosity and wonder. If you take that perspective, things are a lot simpler. Most clients are already assuming that you made decisions on their behalf with all of your knowledge and experience behind them.
